turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Welcome to the show. Today we're exploring how artificial intelligence is transforming drug discovery — a field where a single approved drug can take fifteen years and over two billion dollars to develop. To start us off, what exactly makes traditional drug discovery so expensive and prone to failure?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Think of it as a funnel with a ninety percent failure rate. You invest heavily through target identification, hit discovery, and lead optimization, but fewer than one in ten candidates entering clinical trials actually reaches approval. That two point six billion dollar figure includes the cost of all those failures.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So where does AI actually enter this pipeline to help? Is it trying to fix that failure rate, or is it more about speeding things up before you even get to clinical trials?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: It's doing both. Before clinical trials, AI lets us navigate chemical spaces that were previously unreachable. For example, the Enamine REAL Space contains thirty

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Seven billion synthetically accessible compounds — that's an almost incomprehensible number. How can AI possibly help researchers search through something that vast without having to evaluate every single molecule individually?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Instead of docking each molecule, graph neural networks learn to recognize structural patterns that predict activity. Think of it like learning to identify a sports car—you don't need to test drive every vehicle, you learn the features that define the category and scan for those patterns across the entire library.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's a brilliant analogy. But let's say AI identifies thousands

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: That's a brilliant analogy. But let's say AI identifies thousands...of promising hits. The next bottleneck is ADMET—will the drug actually absorb properly, avoid liver toxicity, not get metabolized too quickly? Graph neural networks can rapidly predict these properties, filtering thousands down to the few worth synthesizing and testing in the lab.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: So AI is essentially acting as an intelligent filter at every stage—finding promising candidates faster and eliminating likely failures before expensive lab work begins. If you had to summarize, where will this field be in ten years?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: In ten years, we'll likely see that 2.6 billion dollar price tag and fifteen-year timeline cut dramatically. Deep learning won't just filter failures faster—it could fundamentally change what's discoverable, finding viable compounds for targets we currently consider "undruggable" because traditional methods simply can't navigate their complexity.
